title: Raspberry jam
subtitle: Open-pan, no pectin sachet.
excerpt: Equal weights of raspberries and jam sugar boiled hard in an open pan until the setting point is reached. Bright, sharp, with a soft set that holds a peak on a chilled saucer. Makes about three 340 ml jars.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136), 'To Make Raspberry Jam'. USDA / NCHFP setting-point guidance: 104.5°C at the surface of the boiling jam in open-pan boiling.

recipe.freezeNotes: Sealed jars keep for 12 months in a cool dark cupboard; no freezer needed.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubling works if your jam pan is large enough. The fruit doubles its surface area; the rolling boil needs room.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sterilise jars and prepare lids before the fruit hits the pan. Once the jam reaches setting point, it has to go into jars immediately.

recipeTools.notes: Wide and shallow; a heavy stockpot works too.
Helpful for the setting point; the cold-saucer test works without one.
Three 340 ml jars with vinegar-proof lids.

glossaryTerms.definition: The stage at which jam reaches the sugar concentration that gels on cooling — 104.5°C in an open pan. The classic cold-saucer test: drop a teaspoon of jam onto a chilled plate, wait 30 seconds, push with a fingertip. If the surface wrinkles, the jam is set.

content.text: Equal weights of raspberries and jam sugar, no added water. The fruit is brought slowly to a simmer while the sugar dissolves; then the pan is brought to a rolling boil that has to be maintained, undisturbed, until the surface temperature reaches 104.5°C. Twenty minutes of boiling, three jars of bright sharp jam.

Raspberries are naturally high in pectin and acid, so jam-sugar (which has added pectin) is optional. Regular caster sugar works too; the set is a touch softer. The open pan matters: a tall narrow pot won't let the boil reduce the water, and the jam won't reach setting point in any reasonable time.

attrs.title: Sterilising the jars
attrs.body: Wash jars and lids in hot soapy water; rinse. Sit upright on a baking tray and dry in a 140°C oven for 15 minutes. Lids the same. Fill jars hot, with hot jam, and seal at once. Cold jars and hot jam crack on contact.

items.prepNote: fresh, hulled, picked over for any squashed fruit
or jam sugar with added pectin

content.text: Spread {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar in a roasting tray and warm in a 140°C oven for 10 minutes. Warm sugar dissolves faster and stops the temperature dropping when it hits the fruit.
Sit {{raspberries}} of raspberries in the jam pan over low heat with {{lemon}} of lemon juice. Cook gently for 5 minutes until the fruit collapses and the juice runs.
Tip the warm sugar into the pan. Stir gently with a wooden spoon until every grain has dissolved. Look closely; sugar that's still in crystals at this stage will crystallise the finished jam.

Bring to a rolling boil over high heat. Boil for 8 to 15 minutes; the time varies with pan width and fruit ripeness. The jam is ready when the surface reads 104.5°C on a sugar thermometer or passes the cold-saucer test. Skim any pink foam from the surface with a metal spoon. The mixture turns from frothy red to glossy deep crimson as it nears the

Take the pan off the heat. Let the jam rest for 10 minutes so the fruit suspends evenly through the jar rather than floating to the top. Ladle into hot sterilised jars; cover with the lids; tighten when warm. Cool fully, label, store in a cool dark cupboard.

items.fix: Tip the jam back into the pan and boil for another 3 to 5 minutes. Test again on a cold saucer.
Sit the open jar in a warm water bath; the jam softens. Next time, take it off the heat at the first wrinkle on the test saucer.

items.symptom: Sugar crystals in the finished jam
items.cause: Sugar not fully dissolved before the rolling boil.
items.fix: Stir patiently until the spoon glides through clear liquid. Warm the sugar in the oven first.

items.symptom: Fruit floats to the top of the jar
items.fix: Rest the jam for 10 minutes; the fruit settles back through the syrup.

content.text: Raspberry jam is the easiest of the British summer preserves — high pectin, high acid, almost incapable of failing for a careful cook. Beeton calls it the housewife's first jam. Spread on hot buttered toast for breakfast, layered into a Victoria sponge, spooned over Greek yoghurt at lunch.
